title: Arnica muscle balm
subtitle: An arnica-infused beeswax balm for tired muscles
excerpt: A beeswax balm built on arnica-infused olive oil, with peppermint and eucalyptus essential oils for a cooling, penetrating quality. Used topically on sore muscles and bruises; not for use on broken skin. The arnica infusion is made 4 weeks ahead.
sourceNotes: Rosemary Gladstar, Herbal Recipes for Vibrant Health (2008), Ch. 8, arnica-infused oil for topical use, contraindications note. Essential Oil Safety, Tisserand and Young (2014), pp. 248, 259, eucalyptus and peppermint dermal limits and pregnancy caution.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles to 200 g. The infusion needs to be made in the scaled quantity; the active session scales directly.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The arnica infusion needs 4 weeks ahead of the balm session. Active work is 20 minutes.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 12 months in a cool, dark location. Anhydrous; no preservative needed. Discard if the balm develops a rancid smell or the essential oil top notes have faded completely.

glossaryTerms.definition: Contains no water. This balm is anhydrous and keeps without a preservative.
A herb or preparation used to support healing of damaged tissue. Arnica is used as a topical vulnerary for bruising and muscle soreness.

content.text: An arnica muscle balm. Olive oil infused with dried arnica flowers for 4 weeks provides the active botanical base; beeswax at 15% sets the balm to a firm, slow-melt consistency suitable for rubbing into sore muscles; peppermint and eucalyptus essential oils provide a cooling, mildly analgesic-feeling sensation on application. The balm is
so it keeps 12 months without a preservative.

Arnica is for external use only and must not be applied to broken skin, open wounds, or mucous membranes. Avoid during pregnancy. Peppermint and eucalyptus essential oils should not be used on or near children under 2 years old. Cats and dogs are highly sensitive to both essential oils; keep the balm stored out of reach of pets.

items.prepNote: infused with dried arnica flowers (Arnica montana) for 4 weeks. Cold-infuse: fill a clean jar with olive oil and dried arnica heads, seal, and leave in a cool dark cupboard for 4 weeks. Strain through muslin before use.
yellow beeswax. 15% of the batch. Sets the balm to a firm rubbing consistency.
Mentha piperita peppermint essential oil. 2% of the batch. Gives the cooling sensation. Add after removing from heat.
Eucalyptus globulus or smithii essential oil. 2% of the batch. Adds a penetrating, decongestant quality.

content.text: Melt the beeswax in the infused oil

Place the beeswax pellets and the arnica-infused olive oil in a heatproof bowl over a saucepan of barely simmering water. Stir until the beeswax has fully melted; the temperature will be around 65-70°C. Remove from the heat.
Add the essential oils and pour

Allow the blend to cool to below 60°C before adding the essential oils; above 60°C the more volatile peppermint notes volatilise off. Stir the peppermint and eucalyptus oils through the melted blend. Pour into the tins before the beeswax starts to cloud. Leave undisturbed for 30 minutes until fully set.

Scoop a small amount of balm onto the fingertips and rub into the affected area with firm circular motions. The beeswax melts on skin contact and the oil absorbs into the skin. Use after exercise or before bed on sore joints, muscles, or bruises. Not for use on the face or broken skin.

Patch-test on the inside of the wrist for 24-48 hours before wider use. Arnica can cause an allergic contact dermatitis in people with Asteraceae family allergies (ragweed, chamomile). Peppermint essential oil at 2% can cause a mild tingling sensation on sensitive skin; reduce to 1% if this is uncomfortable.

12 months in a cool, dark location. Keep the tin lid on when not in use to prevent the essential oil fragrance from volatilising. Discard if the balm develops a rancid smell indicating the olive oil has gone off.
